notes.anchor: I set about composing the \textit{Topics} of Aristotle
notes.note: Cicero composed his own Topica for Trebatius on the voyage of 44 BC, modelling it on Aristotle's Topics, the treatise on dialectical commonplaces; he claims (with some exaggeration) to have written it from memory while at sea.

notes.anchor: can your civil law be learned from books?
notes.note: The ius civile, the body of Roman private law expounded by the jurisconsults; Trebatius's own specialism. Cicero argues that, like his Topics, the law cannot be mastered from texts alone but needs a teacher and practice.

notes.anchor: the loci themselves rising up to meet you
notes.note: Loci (Greek topoi), the 'commonplaces' or standard lines of argument that are the subject of the Topics; Cicero promises that with practice they will spring to mind the instant a question is posed.

notes.anchor: if I return safe and find the state safe
notes.note: Written in late July 44 BC, soon after Caesar's assassination, as Cicero set out toward Greece; the anxious proviso reflects the precarious politics of the year and his uncertainty about returning to Rome.
